_meta.source: ux-skill v2 — Python pivot, landing patterns module

entries.name: Hero — split screen with typewriter code
entries.purpose: Show the product is a developer tool while making the value visceral. Left side narrative, right side live code window typing the install command in cinematic timing.
anatomy: h1 in display serif or display sans
anti_slop_checks: no Inter as display headline
no purple-to-blue gradient
no three-equal-card grid beneath
entries.notes: The typewriter is the wow. Keep it under 5 seconds. After it finishes, pause for 800ms before any other motion fires — restraint reads as premium.

entries.name: Hero — editorial wide single column
entries.purpose: Make the proposition feel inevitable. One enormous headline, one short lede, one CTA — built on a generous baseline grid so the type carries the whole load.
anatomy: h1 in display serif at 96-128px desktop
lede in 18-22px body serif or grotesque
anti_slop_checks: no purple-blue gradient text
no animated emoji confetti
no glass card stacked behind headline
no carousel of features below
entries.notes: If the headline reads at 56px, you're not committing. Display type needs scale to do its job — push it until it feels almost too big, then back off one notch.

entries.name: Hero — full-bleed product photography
entries.purpose: Let the physical object or screen do the selling. Photography occupies the full viewport, type sits in negative space, the whole composition exists to make the product feel desirable.
anatomy: h1 overlay in white or ink depending on tonal zone
states: load (image fades to full + headline slides)
anti_slop_checks: no stock photo of diverse team smiling at laptop
no purple gradient overlay
no 'photo collage' of multiple shots
no glow halo around the product
entries.notes: The photo is the whole pattern. If you have a mediocre render, change the pattern — this one falls apart with anything less than gallery-grade imagery.

entries.name: Hero — bento grid 3x2
entries.purpose: Front-load multiple value props for a multi-feature product. Six asymmetric tiles in a 3x2 grid, each carrying one message, sized by importance.
anatomy: 6 asymmetric bento tiles (large hero tile + 5 supporting)
varying tile content (text, mini-chart, icon, mini-screenshot)
anti_slop_checks: no equal-sized grid (defeats the purpose)
no purple gradients inside tiles
no identical card chrome on every tile
no emoji as tile icons
entries.notes: The trap is making all tiles feel like the same card. Each tile must look like it was designed alone — different content type, different visual density, different anchor.

entries.name: Hero — animated gradient with restraint
entries.purpose: Use motion in the background to add atmosphere without distraction. A slow, low-contrast aurora gradient drifts behind crisp typography.
anatomy: background canvas with animated low-amplitude gradient
h1 in clean sans or display serif
anti_slop_checks: no chrome purple-to-blue gradient
no fast rainbow oscillation
no gradient text overlay on top of gradient bg
no orb that pulses on hover
entries.notes: This pattern is what default AI does. The discipline is restraint — desaturate the gradient until it almost reads as fog, slow the loop until you stop noticing, and never let the gradient become the content.

entries.name: Hero — newsletter / waitlist signup focus
entries.purpose: Single goal page. Convert visitors into a list. Strong headline, one input field, dense social proof beneath to overcome 'why should I' friction.
anatomy: single email input with inline submit button
social proof row (avatars + count, or named subscribers)
anti_slop_checks: no 8-field signup form
no privacy-policy block above the input
no purple gradient button
no auto-popup modal duplicating this same form
entries.notes: Social proof is the load-bearing element. Real subscriber names beat a fake 'join 10,000+ readers' badge every time.

entries.name: Features — 3-up icon row
entries.purpose: Classic mature pattern for surfacing three genuinely distinct pillars. Works only when each feature is qualitatively different — not three flavors of the same thing.
anatomy: icon (custom illustration or 1.5px stroke line icon) per column
anti_slop_checks: no three identical chip-like cards with rounded corners and drop shadows
no purple-blue gradient icons
no emoji as icons
no 'Fast / Reliable / Scalable' generic copy
entries.notes: If you can't write three genuinely distinct feature names, this isn't the right pattern. Use accordion or staggered-scene instead — never pad the third column to make the row balance.

entries.name: Features — staggered scroll-pinned scenes
entries.purpose: Give each feature its own viewport. Pin the section while the visual on one side swaps through synchronized stages. Cinematic, slow, controlled.
anatomy: sticky text column (left or right, swapping copy)
sticky visual column (swapping screenshots or illustrations)
anti_slop_checks: no horizontal-scroll hijack
no auto-advancing carousel beneath the pin
no scroll-jacking that traps the user
no 5+ scenes (3-4 is the upper limit)
entries.notes: Reduced-motion users need a stacked-vertical fallback that ignores the pin entirely. The transition between scenes should feel like a film cut, not a slot machine.

entries.name: Features — pricing-table layout repurposed
entries.purpose: Surface comparative feature differences across personas or tiers when there's no actual pricing yet. The table format does the heavy lifting on contrast.
anatomy: 3 vertical columns with sticky tier-style headers
feature row labels in left rail
checkmarks, dashes, or short text values per cell
optional 'highlighted column' for the recommended path
anti_slop_checks: no purple gradients on the recommended column
no animated checkmarks scaling on scroll
no 'most popular' ribbon flag
no shadow elevation per row
entries.notes: If you're using a pricing table for non-pricing content, label it clearly. Users see a tier table and assume cost — make the column headers describe a user role or workflow, not a plan.

entries.name: Features — accordion / progressive disclosure
entries.purpose: Handle a long feature list without overwhelming. Each feature is a collapsible row; the first one or two are expanded by default to show the pattern.
anatomy: expanded row body (paragraph + optional inline visual)
anti_slop_checks: no purple chevron icon
no card-with-shadow per accordion row
no jumping page height (use auto-height transitions only)
no 30+ rows (cap at 8-12)
entries.notes: The plus-to-minus icon transition is where this pattern lives or dies. Use a single morphing SVG, not two stacked icons fading.

entries.name: Social proof — logo carousel
entries.purpose: Borrowed credibility from recognized brands. Works only with 6+ logos a target audience will recognize on sight.
anatomy: marquee row of logos (continuous loop, slow)
all logos monochrome and tinted to muted gray
edge-fade mask on left and right
anti_slop_checks: no 4 fake logos labeled 'Logo'
no full-color logos (breaks rhythm)
no purple background
no auto-rotating tiles with one logo at a time
entries.notes: If you don't have 6 recognizable logos, skip this pattern entirely. A short row of 4 weak logos reads as 'we couldn't fill the row' and damages trust.

entries.name: Social proof — testimonial editorial
entries.purpose: One genuine quote, presented like a magazine pull-quote. Photo of the actual person, name, title, company. Editorial composition, not a card.
anatomy: large pull-quote in serif italic or display sans
thin hairline beneath or beside the quote
anti_slop_checks: no 3 identical testimonial cards in a row
no 5-star rating row beneath the quote
no purple quote marks
no generic stock-photo headshots
entries.notes: One real quote beats three fake ones. If you only have a paraphrase, say so — attribute it as 'paraphrased from' or rewrite as your own claim with a customer cite.

entries.name: Social proof — case study link wall
entries.purpose: Surface customer outcomes as a wall of compact cards, each carrying one metric and a brand mark. Built to be skimmed.
anatomy: card content: brand logo + headline metric (e.g., '42% lift') + one-line context
optional 'see all case studies' link below grid
anti_slop_checks: no purple-blue gradient on metric numbers
no 5-star ratings on cards
no fake metrics like '10x productivity'
no identical card heights forcing copy padding
entries.notes: The metric is the hero of each card. If you can't lead with a specific number, this isn't the pattern — use the testimonial editorial pattern instead.

entries.name: Pricing — tiered horizontal
entries.purpose: Stripe-style 3-tier comparison. One recommended tier visually emphasized. Side-by-side, scannable, conversion-tuned.
anatomy: tier name + price + brief description per column
recommended column with subtle emphasis (hairline-thicker border or muted tint)
anti_slop_checks: no 'most popular' ribbon flag
no purple recommended column
no animated price counter ticking up
no 5 tiers (3 is the discipline)
entries.notes: The price needs to be readable in one glance. Don't bury it under feature counts — price is the hero, features support the price.

entries.name: Pricing — single CTA with comparison
entries.purpose: One thing to buy. Show the price clearly, then surface comparison context (versus alternative, versus DIY, versus competitor) as supporting structure.
anatomy: comparison block beneath (small table or two-column 'us vs alternative')
trust microcopy (money-back, no card required, etc.)
anti_slop_checks: no 3 fake tier columns padded to look like options
no purple discount banner
no urgency timer (creates distrust)
no competitor logos in the comparison (legal risk)
entries.notes: When there's only one product, don't fake a comparison with yourself. Compare to the real alternative — the status quo, the manual workflow, the open-source DIY path.

entries.name: CTA section — full-bleed dark
entries.purpose: End-of-page conversion zone. Inverted color palette to interrupt the scroll, single primary action, no distractions.
anatomy: single h2 ('Ready to start' style, brief)
single primary CTA in inverted color (light on dark)
no other navigation, no secondary actions
states: scroll-in (text fades up as section enters viewport)
anti_slop_checks: no purple-to-blue gradient background
no glowing particles or orbs
no urgency countdown timer
no testimonial squeezed inside the CTA block
entries.notes: One action. Not 'Sign up or talk to sales.' Pick the action. If you genuinely need both, this isn't the pattern — use embedded form instead.

entries.name: CTA section — embedded form
entries.purpose: Lead-gen oriented. The form lives inline (no modal), 2-4 fields, designed to read as part of the page composition rather than an interruption.
anatomy: optional inline success state replacing the form
states: validation (inline error per field if invalid)
anti_slop_checks: no 8-field form
no purple submit button
no popup confirmation modal (use inline replacement)
no reCAPTCHA badge dominating the section
entries.notes: Every field you add cuts conversion. The honest test: would you fill in this form right now to get the product? If no, cut a field.

entries.name: CTA section — install command card
entries.purpose: Developer tool conversion. Show the literal install command in a code window with a one-click copy button. The act of copying is the conversion.
anatomy: section heading ('Get started in 30 seconds' or similar)
tabs for package managers (npm / pnpm / yarn / bun)
anti_slop_checks: no purple-gradient code window
no animated typing of the install command (keep it static — copy is the action)
no emoji in the command string
no fake 'live downloads counter'
entries.notes: The copy button must give clear feedback that the command is now on the clipboard. Silent copy looks broken — the 'Copied' confirmation is mandatory.

entries.name: Footer — rich grid 4-column
entries.purpose: Mature SaaS information architecture. Multiple link columns (Product / Company / Resources / Legal) grouped by job, plus brand row below.
anatomy: bottom row with copyright + legal links + status pill
states: status-pill (live status color from status API)
anti_slop_checks: no purple gradient background
no large 'Subscribe to our newsletter' block hijacking the footer
no logo in 200px width (keep footer wordmark small)
no social icons in full color
entries.notes: The status pill is a small detail that signals operational maturity. If your service has a public status page, link to it here with a live color dot.

entries.name: Footer — minimal mono
entries.purpose: Single row, hairline-separated, deliberately small. For brands where the footer is plumbing, not real estate.
anti_slop_checks: no social icon row (it bloats the row)
no newsletter input (use a dedicated section, not the footer)
no 'Built with love' badge
no purple anything
entries.notes: The discipline is what you leave out. Resist the urge to add a column 'just in case' — if you need more, you need the 4-column pattern.

entries.name: Footer — newsletter capture + sitemap
entries.purpose: Combine two jobs: harvest emails one last time before users leave, while still surfacing the sitemap. Two-tier composition.
anatomy: top tier (newsletter signup spanning full width)
states: submitted (top tier morphs to thank-you message)
anti_slop_checks: no purple gradient on the newsletter tier
no 'Subscribe now and get 10% off' (treats users as marks)
no auto-popup duplicating the same form
no checkbox pre-checked for marketing consent
entries.notes: Make the post-submit confirmation feel like a genuine thank you. A generic 'You've been subscribed' kills the moment that should reward the action.

entries.name: Navigation — sticky transparent with backdrop blur
entries.purpose: Modern default. Bar floats over content; on scroll it gains a backdrop-filter blur and faint surface tint to preserve legibility without breaking the composition.
anatomy: secondary actions (login, CTA) on the right
optional sub-nav row that appears on hover
states: initial (fully transparent at top of page)
anti_slop_checks: no purple-gradient nav bar
no glass nav that reads as completely opaque (defeats the transparency)
no full-width hover bar across all links
no logo that grows on scroll
entries.notes: Backdrop-filter falls back ungracefully in older browsers — keep the fallback as a faint solid tint, not a missing nav. Test on Safari, Firefox, and Chrome.

entries.name: Navigation — wordmark only with hamburger
entries.purpose: Luxury / brand-first navigation. Wordmark is the only thing on the bar at rest; everything else lives behind a hamburger that opens to a full-screen overlay.
anatomy: overlay contains: large link list + secondary info (address, language switch)
close glyph (X) in the overlay corner
anti_slop_checks: no purple hamburger icon
no 'three lines that morph into X' that overshoots
no auto-open hamburger on first visit
no menu items in small text inside the overlay (go big in the overlay)
entries.notes: The overlay is the whole experience. It must feel like a designed surface, not just a menu list — give it composition, hierarchy, and breathing room.

entries.name: Navigation — command-palette inside
entries.purpose: Developer-tool navigation. The bar carries a search-shaped affordance with a keyboard shortcut hint (⌘K); pressing it opens a command palette that handles nav, search, and actions in one surface.
anatomy: command palette modal (opens on click or shortcut)
palette: search input + filtered result groups (pages, docs, actions)
secondary actions remain in the right of the nav row
anti_slop_checks: no purple palette modal
no fake 'AI search' branding inside the palette
no auto-open palette on first visit
no command palette that doesn't actually navigate (purely cosmetic)
entries.notes: The keyboard shortcut must actually work — if ⌘K doesn't open the palette, the affordance is a lie. Wire it up before you ship the visual.

entries.name: Hero — product screenshot cropped at the fold
entries.purpose: Let the actual product UI carry the proof. Crop a high-fidelity screenshot so it bleeds off the bottom of the hero, signalling there's more to scroll into. Visceral, honest, zero abstraction.
anatomy: h1 in clean sans or display serif
large product screenshot anchored to the bottom of the section, top-cropped to bleed off-fold
anti_slop_checks: no glassy floating browser chrome with traffic-light dots and purple glow
no 3D-tilted product screenshot floating in space
no fake cursor pointing at fake UI
no purple gradient halo behind the screenshot
entries.notes: The screenshot must be a real screen, captured at 2x retina, with real data — not a Figma mock with lorem ipsum. The crop is the discipline: don't show the whole UI, show enough to make people scroll.

entries.name: Hero — marquee of customer logos
entries.purpose: Lead with borrowed credibility. Put a continuously scrolling marquee of recognizable customer logos directly under the headline, before any product visual. The proposition is 'these brands chose us' before it's 'here is what we do.'
anatomy: marquee row of monochrome logos directly beneath CTAs
anti_slop_checks: no 4 weak logos labeled 'Acme', 'Globex', 'Initech', 'Hooli'
no full-color logos breaking the rhythm
no purple gradient bar containing the marquee
no logos at random heights creating a sawtooth row
entries.notes: Default-AI for B2B landing pages. The trap is shipping with placeholder logos to 'fill the marquee' — that reads as cargo-cult social proof and damages trust. If you don't have 8+ logos a B2B buyer recognizes, do not use this pattern.

entries.name: Features — bento grid
entries.purpose: Surface 4-8 distinct value props in a single asymmetric grid where tile size encodes importance. The Apple-iPhone influence on 2024-25 SaaS marketing — each tile is its own designed composition.
anatomy: per-tile content varies (chart, screenshot, illustration, big-number metric, terminal output)
tile titles in small caps or sentence case
subtle hairline between tiles, no card shadows
states: stagger-in (each tile fades up sequentially on scroll)
anti_slop_checks: no equal-sized tile grid (defeats the bento entirely)
no identical card chrome on every tile
no purple-to-blue gradient on every tile
no AI-generated abstract blob art as tile content
no emoji as tile icons
entries.notes: The most-copied 2024-25 SaaS pattern. The bento works only when each tile is genuinely different in content type — if all 6 tiles are 'icon + heading + paragraph,' you've built a 3-up grid that happens to have weird sizes. Vary the content type per tile.

entries.name: Features — stepped product tour
entries.purpose: Walk the user through the product's flow as a numbered narrative — step 1, step 2, step 3 — each step paired with a screenshot or short loop video. Reads like an illustrated story.
anatomy: vertical stack of step rows (typically 3-5 steps)
each row: large step numeral (01, 02, 03) + step title + body + paired visual
alternating left/right visual placement per row (zig-zag)
states: active (current step visual auto-plays when in viewport)
anti_slop_checks: no purple gradient step numerals
no checkmark instead of step number (loses the narrative)
no animated arrow snaking between steps
no auto-advancing carousel replacing the vertical stack
entries.notes: The step numerals are the load-bearing typography — make them big, confident, and the heaviest weight on the section. If the user has to hunt for which step they're on, the narrative breaks.

entries.name: Social proof — magazine quote rail
entries.purpose: A horizontal rail of pull-quotes from press / publications (TechCrunch, Wired, NYT, etc.), each treated like a magazine clipping with publication logo and short excerpt. Curated press wall.
anatomy: section eyebrow ('In the press' or similar)
horizontal scroll rail of 4-6 quote cards
per card: publication wordmark (top), italic pull-quote (2-3 lines), attribution (writer name, date)
subtle hairline cards, no drop shadows
anti_slop_checks: no 5-star ratings appended to press quotes
no purple gradient on quote text
no fake publication names ('Tech Daily', 'Business Weekly')
no AI-generated cover-image collages behind the rail
entries.notes: Real press wins only — if the only quote you have is from your own podcast, this isn't the pattern. The publication wordmarks should be in their actual house style (monochrome tinted to muted gray), never recreated as 'inspired by.'

entries.name: Social proof — numbers counter strip
entries.purpose: A horizontal band of 3-5 huge metrics — customers served, transactions processed, time saved, money moved. The metrics carry the persuasion; the rest of the band is just spacing.
anatomy: full-width section, typically inverted (dark or muted canvas)
per column: oversized numeral (96-160px) + short label (1-2 lines)
metric numerals optionally animate from 0 to value on scroll-in
anti_slop_checks: no purple-to-blue gradient on numerals
no fake 'live counter' that keeps ticking after page load
no metrics with absurd claims (10x productivity, infinite scale)
no decimal-point precision theater ('99.997% uptime') without source
entries.notes: Classic AI-generated section. The discipline is honest, specific, sourceable numbers — every metric needs a footnote or context, or it reads as marketing fluff. If you have to round to a 'nice' number, you don't have the number.

entries.name: Pricing — usage-based slider
entries.purpose: For consumption-priced products (API calls, GB of storage, seats, minutes). A horizontal slider lets the visitor model their expected usage; the price recalculates live. Conveys 'pay for what you use.'
anatomy: labeled slider (min - max, with tick marks)
states: edge (slider at min or max, hint reveals enterprise contact)
anti_slop_checks: no purple gradient slider track
no jitter on the price during drag (debounce the tween)
no fake usage tier 'recommendations' that all suggest the highest plan
no surprise overage fees hidden in fine print beneath the slider
entries.notes: The slider must show the user feeling 'in control' — they drag, the price moves, no surprises. If your pricing has overages or floors, surface them in the breakdown, not in a footnote.

entries.name: Pricing — enterprise call-only
entries.purpose: When the product is enterprise-grade and pricing is bespoke. No public price; a single block invites the buyer to a sales conversation, with credibility cues (logos, security certifications) replacing the price tag.
anatomy: section heading ('Built for enterprise' or similar)
value-prop bullet list (3-5 items relevant to enterprise: SSO, SLA, custom contracts, dedicated success)
single primary CTA ('Talk to sales' or 'Request a demo')
anti_slop_checks: no fake 'starting at $X' price that nobody actually pays
no urgency timer for an enterprise contact form
no purple gradient enterprise badge
no 'limited slots available' scarcity theater
entries.notes: The CTA is everything. 'Contact us' is weak — name the action you actually want ('Book a 30-minute demo,' 'Talk to a solutions engineer'). The buyer should know exactly what happens after they click.

entries.name: CTA — inline email capture (no modal)
entries.purpose: Convert without interruption. A single email input sits inline in the page composition — no overlay, no modal, no slide-in. The user types, submits, sees confirmation in place.
anatomy: single email input with inline submit button (button inside the input or anchored right)
trust microcopy beneath (privacy, frequency, no spam)
inline success state that replaces the input on submit
states: success (input morphs to thank-you message in place)
anti_slop_checks: no auto-popup modal duplicating this same input on the same page
no exit-intent popup hijacking the same conversion
no purple gradient submit button
no 'subscribe' button (be specific — 'Get the newsletter,' 'Join the list')
entries.notes: The promise of 'no modal' must be kept everywhere on the page — if the same email capture also auto-pops on scroll, you've lied to the user. One inline capture, end of story.

entries.name: CTA — time-bound promo banner
entries.purpose: Top-of-page or section-anchored banner announcing a time-limited offer (early-bird pricing, Black Friday, launch week). Used to drive urgency for a real deadline.
anatomy: thin full-width banner (top of page or anchored above CTA section)
left: short headline ('Launch week — 30% off annual')
close (X) glyph at far right to dismiss
states: expired (timer at 0:0:0, banner morphs to 'Offer ended' or hides)
anti_slop_checks: no fake countdown that resets on every page load
no countdown to a deadline that never actually triggers a price change
no purple-to-red urgency gradient
no banner that re-appears after the user dismissed it
no scarcity theater ('only 3 spots left!')
entries.notes: The deadline must be real, and the user must be able to see what happens after it expires. Fake urgency is the canonical dark pattern — if the countdown doesn't end at a real cliff, do not use this pattern.

entries.name: Footer — vertical link tree
entries.purpose: Single-column vertical footer for portfolio sites, personal sites, indie products, link-in-bio pages. Every link stacks in one column; the whole footer reads top to bottom like a contact card.
anatomy: vertical list of links (4-8, each on its own row)
social or contact row (single line of inline icons or text links)
anti_slop_checks: no 4-column sitemap (defeats the vertical reading)
no purple gradient name at the top
no 'Built with' or 'Made with' badges (they belong nowhere)
no auto-typing animation on the tagline
entries.notes: The vertical rhythm is the design. Use consistent row height and let the whitespace between rows do the visual hierarchy — don't pad with icons or badges.

entries.name: Navigation — anchored search bar
entries.purpose: Documentation sites, knowledge bases, large catalog pages. A persistent search input is the centerpiece of the nav — wider than a typical command-palette trigger, treated as a primary affordance.
anatomy: wide search input (center, 40-50% of nav width)
search input shows placeholder + ⌘K kbd hint + slash-key kbd hint
result dropdown (opens beneath input on type)
right: secondary actions (sign in, GitHub link, version selector)
states: focus (input ring grows, results panel opens)
typing (results filter live, grouped by section)
anti_slop_checks: no purple gradient search input
no AI-search branding ('Ask AI anything') without an actual LLM behind it
no auto-opening result panel on page load
no full-width hijack covering the page when results open
entries.notes: The search must actually be fast and relevance-ranked. A sluggish or fuzzy search that returns 200 irrelevant results does more damage than not having search at all — wire up Algolia, Meilisearch, or equivalent before shipping this pattern.

entries.name: Trust — SOC2 / ISO / HIPAA badge row
entries.purpose: Surface compliance certifications as a horizontal row of badges. Lives near pricing, enterprise sections, or footer. Signals the product has done the procurement homework an enterprise buyer needs to see.
anatomy: section eyebrow ('Built for compliance' or similar)
horizontal row of certification badges (SOC 2 Type II, ISO 27001, HIPAA, GDPR, PCI DSS as relevant)
badges in monochrome or original certifier colors
optional 'See our security page' link beneath the row
states: hover (badge surface tints, tooltip reveals certifier details)
click (navigate to dedicated security page or external certifier listing)
anti_slop_checks: no fake or 'inspired by' badges that don't link to actual certifier records
no 'In progress' badge mixed in with real certifications
no purple gradient backdrop behind the row
no animated badge spin or pulse
entries.notes: Every badge must be a real, current certification — and clicking the badge should link to the certifier's public record or your security page that does. Decorative compliance badges are a regulatory exposure, not just bad taste.

entries.name: Trust — customer count as trust signal
entries.purpose: A single, prominent number signalling scale or longevity — 'Trusted by 12,000 teams,' '$2.4B processed,' '8 years in production.' Reads as a sentence, not a stat card. Lives near CTAs.
anatomy: single-line sentence with one large number embedded mid-sentence
number set in display weight, 1.5-2x surrounding text size
optional sparkline or trend arrow inline (subtle)
states: scroll-in (sentence fades up, number subtly emphasized)
anti_slop_checks: no '10x' or 'infinite' instead of a specific number
no decimal-point precision theater without a source
no animated counter that loops continuously
no purple gradient on the number
entries.notes: Specific beats round. '11,847 teams' reads as real; '12,000+' reads as rounded marketing. If you can cite the source (a customer dashboard, a billing system, an annual report), say so in the microcopy — that's where the trust actually lives.

entries.name: Onboarding — step zero with first prompt
entries.purpose: For AI-products, code editors, design tools. The 'sign up' page is itself the product — the user lands in a working interface, prefilled with a starter prompt or example, and the first action is to interact, not to fill a form.
anatomy: minimal header (wordmark + 'Already have an account? Sign in')
the product interface itself as the page body (chat, editor, canvas, etc.)
starter prompt or example prefilled with a placeholder
primary action button labeled with the user's first verb ('Try this prompt,' 'Run this code,' 'Render this scene')
soft account creation prompt only after the first interaction succeeds
states: interacting (user types or clicks, product responds)
first-success (result rendered, soft sign-up prompt appears)
signed-up (transition into authenticated session, preserving the work just done)
anti_slop_checks: no 'Sign up to try' wall blocking the demo
no purple gradient on the starter prompt
no fake 'AI is typing' theater when nothing is actually happening
no forced credit-card capture before first interaction
entries.notes: The starter prompt is the make-or-break. It must be specific enough to be impressive when answered, generic enough that any user could relate. 'Write me a poem' is weak; 'Refactor this Express route to use async/await' is strong. Tailor the prompt to your audience.

entries.name: Onboarding — empty state with 3 paths
entries.purpose: First post-signup screen offering three meaningful starting paths instead of a blank canvas. Common in SaaS dashboards, design tools, project managers, CRMs. Resolves the empty-state paralysis with curated entry points.
anatomy: supporting one-liner setting context ('Three ways to get started')
3 path cards (typically: start blank, use a template, import existing)
per card: icon (1.5px stroke), title, 2-line body, primary verb at bottom
tertiary 'skip for now' link beneath the cards
states: hover (card lifts, primary verb gains underline)
skip (route to blank dashboard with persistent help nudge)
anti_slop_checks: no purple-to-blue gradient on the recommended card
no animated illustration of cartoon characters celebrating the user
no 'most popular' ribbon on the middle card
no 5+ paths (3 is the discipline)
no AI-generated abstract blob art on card backgrounds
entries.notes: The three paths must lead to genuinely different next experiences. If 'Start blank' and 'Use a template' both drop the user in the same editor with the same empty state, you've offered fake choice — collapse to one path or make the templates actually do something different.
